caveats: a: BLS occupation evidence is for broad NAICS 713 rather than six-digit marinas.
a: The range concerns current not-yet-automated tasks and may overstate opportunity at marinas already using integrated management systems.
a: The frozen compensation ratio has a 2024-wage/2022-receipts vintage mismatch and is harmonized to the IPS basis as provided.
rho: Vendor feature availability is not evidence of adoption or realized labor savings.
rho: Small seasonal marinas may lack clean data, management capacity, and reliable connectivity.
rho: Implementation excludes pricing, demand, and valuation effects.
e: No source measures eligibility within the supplied LMM population.
e: NAICS permits related fuel, retail, rental, and repair activities, creating firm-level revenue-mix variation.
e: The supplied n of 67 is itself a margin-bridged estimate from size-class data.
s5: The transaction denominator is operations rather than firms and is not limited to the EBITDA band.
s5: Broker reports may miss private transfers and may include real-estate transactions without operating-company control.
s5: The 51% owner-age statistic is for US small businesses generally, not marinas.
q: The 2026 investment report is broker-authored and does not measure AI benefit retention.
q: Pricing power is location-specific; marinas with deferred maintenance or weak amenities can have vacancies despite broader capacity constraints.
q: The estimate excludes volume effects, which are represented in demand primitives.
d5: Revenue and expenditure growth are nominal and not direct quantity measures.
d5: NMMA manufacturer sentiment is North American and adjacent to, not representative of, marina operators.
d5: Climate events, drought, insurance withdrawal, and permitting constraints can produce severe local divergence.
o: Some transient booking, payment, access control, and customer communication can become self-service.
o: This is a bounded judgment, not a measured industry share.
o: Physical operator requirements vary by facility design, fuel sales, repair activity, and local regulation.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Boat owners pay annual, seasonal, monthly, or transient berth and storage charges, often supplemented by launch, utility, fuel, repair, maintenance, and retail revenue.
lens.excluded_activities: Municipal or member-owned facilities without transferable control, captive resort facilities, passive real-estate or financing vehicles, and businesses primarily classified as boat dealers, rental operators, or standalone repair shops.
lens.included_activities: US marina firms in the lower-middle-market band that earn recurring or repeat revenue from wet-slip dockage, dry storage, launching, utilities, fuel, boatyard service, and related services for pleasure-craft owners.

caveats: BLS occupation evidence is for broad NAICS 713 rather than six-digit marinas.
The range concerns current not-yet-automated tasks and may overstate opportunity at marinas already using integrated management systems.
The frozen compensation ratio has a 2024-wage/2022-receipts vintage mismatch and is harmonized to the IPS basis as provided.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task inventory from 15-25 independent US marinas, mapped to employee hours in reservations, accounting, service writing, parts, dock operations, maintenance, and management, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: A wage-weighted bounded judgment assigns meaningful exposure to reservations, billing, customer communication, estimates, scheduling, inventory records, bookkeeping, and routine marketing, while most dock handling, fueling, maintenance, inspections, security, and customer-facing exception work remains physical. BLS shows the broader NAICS 713 workforce is dominated by frontline recreation roles, while current marina software demonstrates that a narrower administrative layer can already be digitized.

d5.bridge: Sources measure adjacent quantities rather than constant-price marina-service volume: a broker report cites 2025 marina revenue growth of 4.3%, roughly 92% occupancy, and more than half of surveyed US marinas above 95% occupancy; NMMA reported 2024 new-powerboat unit sales down 9%-12% but expected 2025 boating expenditures 3%-5% above 2024, and its March 2026 manufacturer survey found 43% expecting modest industry improvement. The range strips out nominal price growth, gives more weight to installed boats and storage occupancy than volatile new-boat sales, and cautiously extends those signals over five years. It may err upward if affordability reduces the installed boating population, or downward where fixed shoreline supply and larger-vessel storage needs bind.
caveats: Revenue and expenditure growth are nominal and not direct quantity measures.
NMMA manufacturer sentiment is North American and adjacent to, not representative of, marina operators.
Climate events, drought, insurance withdrawal, and permitting constraints can produce severe local divergence.
d5.change_evidence: A location-matched five-year dataset of occupied wet slips, dry-stack turns, boat registrations by length, waitlists, churn, and inflation-adjusted service units would materially tighten the range.
d5.rationale: Marina demand is supported by a large installed base, scarce slips in strong markets, and repeat storage needs, but discretionary boat ownership remains sensitive to interest rates, income, fuel costs, insurance, and regional water conditions. Constant-quality service quantity is therefore modeled as roughly stable to modestly growing rather than extrapolating nominal revenue.

caveats: No source measures eligibility within the supplied LMM population.
NAICS permits related fuel, retail, rental, and repair activities, creating firm-level revenue-mix variation.
The supplied n of 67 is itself a margin-bridged estimate from size-class data.
e.change_evidence: A coded review of the supplied 67 estimated LMM firms using ownership type, revenue mix, customer contracts, EBITDA provenance, and control-transferability would directly identify the eligible share.
e.rationale: The industry's core products are external-customer dockage, launching, utilities, and storage, including annual membership and transient fees, so most firms in the supplied EBITDA band fit the recurring-service lens. The deduction allows for member-owned clubs, public or captive facilities, real-estate-heavy shells, and mixed businesses whose transferable economics primarily sit outside marina operations.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 713930 ($1,930,519,385) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 713930 ($6,207,826,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1664 (margins.json[71]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Recreation EBITDA/Sales 16.64%); revenue/employee $200,046 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 713930); class <5: 1,897 firms, avg revenue/firm $530,697 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $88,308 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 706 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,414,824 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $235,427 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 461 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,905,254 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $483,434 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 311 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,784,325 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $962,512 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 46 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,221,609 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,867,276 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 21 firms, avg revenue/firm $26,085,095 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,340,560 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 67

caveats: Some transient booking, payment, access control, and customer communication can become self-service.
This is a bounded judgment, not a measured industry share.
Physical operator requirements vary by facility design, fuel sales, repair activity, and local regulation.
o.change_evidence: Operating data from highly automated marinas showing changes in on-site staffing, unattended transactions, incident rates, regulatory staffing requirements, and outsourced maintenance would test how much demand can become self-service.
o.rationale: The service basket centers on controlled waterfront access, physical berths and storage, launching, utilities, fueling, maintenance coordination, site safety, and exception handling. Customer portals and automated scheduling can reduce touch labor, but they do not eliminate the accountable operator responsible for the facility, assets, environmental compliance, and physical service delivery.

caveats: The 2026 investment report is broker-authored and does not measure AI benefit retention.
Pricing power is location-specific; marinas with deferred maintenance or weak amenities can have vacancies despite broader capacity constraints.
The estimate excludes volume effects, which are represented in demand primitives.
q.change_evidence: Contract samples and five-year cohorts of berth renewal pricing, concessions, occupancy, churn, and operating margin from 20 marinas before and after workflow automation would directly measure retained gross benefit.
q.rationale: Annual, seasonal, monthly, and transient dockage contracts make labor savings less mechanically pass-through than cost-plus billing. Reported high occupancy, waitlists, and operators' ability to raise rates support retention, while renewal repricing, local competition, customer-service expectations, and reinvestment in aging docks and amenities still share part of the benefit with customers or absorb it operationally.

caveats: Vendor feature availability is not evidence of adoption or realized labor savings.
Small seasonal marinas may lack clean data, management capacity, and reliable connectivity.
Implementation excludes pricing, demand, and valuation effects.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after implementation studies from DockMaster or a comparable vendor reporting user-level adoption, hours saved by workflow, error rates, and 24-month persistence across independent marinas would materially move the range.
rho.rationale: Existing marina platforms cover reservations, billing, accounting, service estimates, scheduling, payments, inventory, e-signatures, and mobile work tracking, so much of the exposed office workflow is technically implementable. Five-year realization remains constrained by fragmented legacy data, seasonal rollout windows, weak dock connectivity, staff adoption, integration cost, and the need for human approval of safety-, credit-, and repair-related exceptions.

s5.bridge: The source population is reported US marina properties/operations, not eligible LMM firms: the 2026 investment report lists 102 marina sales in 2025 and 3,408 active marina operations, an annual property-sale incidence near 3.0%. Compounding that incidence for five years gives about 14%; the base is rounded upward to 16% because industry reporting also identifies retirement and consolidation as active sale motives. This can overstate firm control transfers when portfolios contain multiple properties or 2025 activity is unusually strong, and understate them when private deals are absent from the broker dataset.
caveats: The transaction denominator is operations rather than firms and is not limited to the EBITDA band.
Broker reports may miss private transfers and may include real-estate transactions without operating-company control.
The 51% owner-age statistic is for US small businesses generally, not marinas.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year, deduplicated transaction census matching sold marina properties to ultimate firm parents, deal type, control percentage, EBITDA, and prior ownership would replace the proxy with a directly observed transfer rate.
s5.rationale: Observable marina sales, corporate consolidation, individual and regional buyers, and retirement-driven sellers establish a functioning transfer market. The five-year probability remains bounded below general owner-retirement pressure because some exits are family or internal transitions, closures, or nonqualifying property sales.

principal_driver: Recurring, capacity-constrained storage and dockage revenue gives an operator room to retain administrative efficiency gains.
principal_weakness: The automation opportunity is bounded because most essential marina work is physical, safety-sensitive, and site-specific.

sources.supports: Defines marina products as pleasure-craft dockage, launching, utilities, and storage and states that dockage revenue includes annual membership, initiation, and transient fees; supports the recurring-service lens, eligibility, billing structure, and physical operator role.
sources.title: NAPCS Product List for NAICS 7139: Other Amusement and Recreation Industries

sources.supports: Reports broader-subsector occupational employment concentrated in frontline recreation roles, including 276,700 amusement and recreation attendants in 2025; used only as an occupation-mix boundary for the automation estimate.
sources.title: Amusement, Gambling, and Recreation Industries: NAICS 713

sources.supports: Documents currently available marina workflows for AI-assisted scheduling and estimates, reservations, billing, accounting, inventory, payments, e-signatures, customer records, and mobile field work; supports task exposure and technical implementability, not realized savings.
sources.title: DockMaster Marina Management Software

sources.supports: Reports 3,408 active marina operations, 102 marina sales in 2025, 2025 revenue up 4.3%, occupancy around 92%, more than half of surveyed US marinas above 95% occupancy, 26% at capacity, and 68% raising rates by 4%-6%; supports transfer, capture, and demand proxies.
sources.title: 2026 Marina Investment Report

sources.supports: A marina broker with more than 250 reported transactions describes retirement among common sale motives and active corporate, individual, and regional acquisition demand; supports the existence and composition of the transfer market.
sources.title: State of the Marina Market

sources.supports: Reports that 51% of US small-business owners are over age 55 and describes sale, family succession, internal succession, and closure as alternative exit paths; used as a broad-population succession proxy.
sources.title: Starting a small business is hard. Exiting can be even harder, but planning early is the key

sources.supports: Reports 2024 US new-powerboat unit sales down an estimated 9%-12%, expected 2025 boating expenditures 3%-5% above 2024, and an estimated 85 million annual US boating participants; supports demand range and cyclicality.
sources.title: Innovation Driving U.S. Boat Sales Demand As Key Winter Boat Show Buying Season Gets Underway

sources.supports: Reports that 43% of surveyed NMMA manufacturer executives expected modest industry improvement and 60% expected company revenue growth over the following year, while infrastructure constraints remained a concern; used as adjacent demand sentiment.
sources.title: Marine Leadership Barometer: Q1 Survey Signals Steadier Industry Outlook in 2026

title: Marinas
